1.template: he having been taught in the language of the {0}

1.title: Why Lehi could read the plates
1.text: Benjamin notes that Lehi had been ‘taught in the language of the Egyptians,’ which let him read the brass plates—a clue to why the Nephites engraved their records in a form of Egyptian rather than Hebrew.

1.question: Why did Benjamin say the brass plates were so important?
options: Without them they would have dwindled in unbelief like the Lamanites
They were made of pure gold
They predicted the exact day of Christ's birth
They could heal the sick

1.template: he should confer the {0} upon one of his sons

1.question: Why did Benjamin have Mosiah send out a proclamation?
options: To gather the people so Benjamin could proclaim Mosiah king
To declare war on the Lamanites
To collect a tax for the temple
To exile the wicked from the land

1.question: What did Benjamin say he would give the people?
options: A name, never to be blotted out except through transgression
A new homeland across the sea
A share of the king's gold
Freedom from all laws

1.title: A name not yet given
1.text: Benjamin promises a special name for his people but doesn't reveal it here. He will give it at the end of his sermon (Mosiah 5): the people take upon themselves the name of Christ.

pairs.right: The scriptures and records of the Jews
The record of the Nephite people
Led Lehi's family through the wilderness

1.title: The relics of the kings
1.text: Along with the records and the sword of Laban, Benjamin entrusts the ‘ball or director’—the Liahona that guided Lehi's family through the wilderness (1 Nephi 16)—passed down with the kingship for centuries.

1.template: to go up to the {0} to hear the words

2.question: Why did the people gather at the temple and offer sacrifice?
options: To give thanks to God and hear king Benjamin
To crown a new high priest
To trade goods at a market
To choose between two kings

2.title: Tents facing the temple
2.text: So many came that they camped by families around the temple, each tent's door turned toward it—so they could hear the king's words from where they sat. The crowd was too large to count.

2.template: he caused a {0} to be erected

2.question: Because the crowd was too large to hear, what did Benjamin do?
options: Had his words written and sent to those out of earshot
Repeated the speech the next day
Spoke only to the elders
Called the people closer inside the temple

2.template: that ye should not be laden with {0}

2.question: How does Benjamin describe his own reign as king?
options: He served the people, sought no riches, and labored with his own hands
He grew rich from heavy taxes
He ruled from afar through servants
He demanded to be worshipped

2.template: ye are only in the service of your {0}

2.title: ‘In the service of your God’
2.text: One of the most quoted lines in the Book of Mormon: ‘when ye are in the service of your fellow beings ye are only in the service of your God.’ Benjamin ties everyday service to worship itself.

2.question: From his own example as a serving king, what does Benjamin teach?
options: If their king labors to serve them, they should serve one another
Kings deserve special honors
Service should be repaid with gold
Only priests need to serve

2.question: Why does Benjamin say we remain forever indebted to God?
options: As soon as he blesses us for obeying, we owe him again
Because we borrowed gold from him
Because the kings recorded our debts
We are not indebted—he owes us

2.template: that I might rid my garments of your {0}

2.question: What does Benjamin declare at the end of this part of his speech?
options: That his son Mosiah is now king and ruler
That he will reign fifty more years
That the temple must be rebuilt
That the Lamanites are coming to war

2.template: beware lest there shall arise {0} among you

2.question: What does Benjamin warn about the one who obeys the evil spirit and dies in his sins?
options: He drinks damnation and receives everlasting punishment
He simply ceases to exist
He is given another life to try again
He becomes king of the Lamanites

2.template: for he dwelleth not in unholy {0}

2.question: What happens to the man who dies an unrepentant enemy to God?
options: Divine justice awakens his guilt, and mercy has no claim on him
He is forgiven automatically at death
He is reborn as a child
Nothing—judgment was already past

2.template: dwell with God in a state of never-ending {0}

3.title: An angel's message
3.text: Benjamin says the words about Christ he is sharing were given to him by an angel of God who woke him in the night—prophecy delivered roughly 124 years before Jesus' birth.

3.question: What did the angel say the Lord Omnipotent would do among men?
options: Heal the sick, raise the dead, and make the blind see
Build a temple of gold
Conquer every nation by the sword
Reign in Jerusalem as an earthly king

3.title: Blood from every pore
3.text: Centuries before Gethsemane, the angel foretold that the Lord's anguish for the people's sins would be so great that ‘blood cometh from every pore’—one of scripture's most vivid prophecies of the Atonement's suffering.

3.question: According to the angel, what would the Lord do on the third day?
options: Rise from the dead
Return to heaven in a chariot
Choose twelve kings
Destroy his enemies with fire

3.question: To whom did God send holy prophets to declare these things?
options: To every kindred, nation, and tongue
Only to the Nephites
Only to the kings
To the Lamanites alone

3.template: the law of Moses availeth nothing except it were through the {0} of his blood

3.question: By what means alone can salvation come, according to the angel?
options: Only in and through the name of Christ
Through keeping the law of Moses
Through the king's blessing
Through offering enough sacrifices

3.template: putteth off the natural man and becometh a {0} through the atonement of Christ

3.title: Putting off the natural man
3.text: Benjamin's teaching that ‘the natural man is an enemy to God’ unless he yields to the Holy Spirit and becomes ‘as a child—submissive, meek, humble, patient, full of love’ is among the most cited passages on conversion.

3.question: When knowledge of the Savior spreads to every nation, who alone are found blameless before God?
options: Little children
Kings and priests
Those who keep the law of Moses
The prophets only

3.template: because of his partaking of the {0} fruit

4.question: When the people fell to the earth, what did they cry out?
options: For mercy and the atoning blood of Christ to forgive their sins
For Benjamin to reign forever
For deliverance from the Lamanites
For rain upon their fields

4.title: Fallen to the earth
4.text: Benjamin's words were so powerful that the whole multitude fell to the earth, seeing themselves as ‘less than the dust,’ and cried out together for the atoning blood of Christ—a collective conversion rare in scripture.

4.template: awakened you to a sense of your {0}
there is none other {0} save this which hath been spoken of

4.question: After believing in God, what does Benjamin say to do?
options: Repent of your sins, forsake them, and humble yourselves before God
Build an altar of stone
Offer up gold and silver
Choose a new king

4.question: What does Benjamin promise those who remember God's greatness and their own nothingness?
options: They will always rejoice, be filled with God's love, and retain a remission of sins
They will gain great riches
They will never face enemies
They will live without working

4.template: ye will not suffer your children that they go hungry, or {0}
ye will not suffer that the {0} putteth up his petition to you in vain

4.question: Why does Benjamin say no one should despise the beggar?
options: Because we are all beggars, dependent on God for everything
Because beggars are secretly wealthy
Because the law forbids speaking to them
Because the king will repay any gift

4.title: ‘Are we not all beggars?’
4.text: Benjamin's question—‘are we not all beggars?’—turns charity into humility: since we depend on God for breath, food, and everything we have, refusing the poor is refusing what was never truly ours.

4.question: What does Benjamin tell the poor who have nothing to give?
options: Say in your heart, ‘If I had, I would give,’ and remain guiltless
Borrow gold to give to others
Give nothing and feel no concern
Beg from the rich instead

4.template: a man should run faster than he has {0}

4.question: What does Benjamin counsel at the close of his sermon?
options: Watch yourselves—your thoughts, words, and deeds—and keep the commandments
Build a wall around the city
Appoint judges over the land
Store up gold for hard times

4.title: Not faster than he has strength
4.text: Benjamin's counsel that ‘it is not requisite that a man should run faster than he has strength’ but must be diligent and do all things ‘in wisdom and order’ is often quoted as scriptural wisdom on pacing and balance.

5.template: which has wrought a mighty {0} in us, or in our hearts

5.title: A mighty change of heart
5.text: The people testify that the Spirit ‘wrought a mighty change’ in them, so they had ‘no more disposition to do evil, but to do good continually’—one of the Book of Mormon's clearest descriptions of conversion.

5.question: After the mighty change of heart, what were the people willing to do?
options: Enter a covenant to do God's will and obey His commandments
Crown a new king at once
March against the Lamanites
Divide the land among themselves

5.question: Because of the covenant, what does Benjamin say the people are now called?
options: The children of Christ—his sons and daughters, spiritually begotten
The kings of Zarahemla
The children of Lehi
A new tribe of Israel

5.template: I would that ye should take upon you the {0} of Christ

5.title: The promised name revealed
5.text: The name Benjamin foretold in Mosiah 1—one ‘never to be blotted out except through transgression’—is now revealed: the people take upon themselves the name of Christ, becoming His sons and daughters.

5.template: retain the name written always in your {0}

5.question: How does Benjamin counsel the people to remain, so Christ may seal them His?
options: Steadfast and immovable, always abounding in good works
Silent and hidden from the world
Wealthy and powerful
Separated into many groups

6.question: After the address, how many of the people had entered the covenant and taken Christ's name?
options: Every soul except the little children
Only the priests and elders
Just half of the multitude
None until the next day

6.title: The names were recorded
6.text: Benjamin had the names of all who entered the covenant written down, then consecrated Mosiah king and appointed priests—formally organizing the people around the covenant they had just made.

6.template: he began to reign in the {0} year of his age

6.title: Counting from Lehi
6.text: Mormon marks time by Lehi: Mosiah began to reign about 476 years after Lehi left Jerusalem (around 124 BC). The Nephites still dated their history from that founding journey.

6.question: Like his father Benjamin, what did king Mosiah do so as not to burden his people?
options: He tilled the earth himself
He lowered all taxes to nothing
He gave away the royal treasury
He appointed many new kings

7.title: The lost colony
7.text: Three years into Mosiah's peaceful reign, his people were so curious about the colony that had left for the land of Lehi-Nephi—and never been heard from—that they ‘wearied him’ until he sent an expedition to find them.

7.question: What did king Mosiah do about the people who had gone to the land of Lehi-Nephi?
options: Sent sixteen strong men, led by Ammon, to find them
Declared them lost forever
Sent an army to conquer them
Forbade anyone to speak of them

7.question: Who was the king that Ammon's party met in the land of Nephi?
options: Limhi, the son of Noah, son of Zeniff
King Benjamin's brother
The Lamanite king Laman
Mosiah himself

7.title: Three generations of kings
7.text: King Limhi traces a three-generation line of Nephite kings in the land of Lehi-Nephi: Zeniff, his son Noah, and Limhi himself—a colony cut off from Zarahemla for decades.

7.question: What did king Limhi reveal about his people's condition?
options: They were in bondage to the Lamanites, paying a grievous tribute
They had grown rich and powerful
They had forgotten the Nephite language
They lived in perfect peace

7.question: Which past deliverances did Limhi recall to give his people hope?
options: God led Israel out of Egypt, through the Red Sea, and fed them manna
The conquest of Jericho
The reign of King David
The building of Solomon's temple

7.question: How did Zeniff's people first fall into bondage?
options: Zeniff, over-zealous, was deceived by a treaty with king Laman
They surrendered after losing a great battle
They sold themselves to pay debts
A famine forced them to submit

7.template: to the amount of {0} of our corn
a {0} of the Lord have they slain

7.question: Why does Limhi say his people were brought into bondage?
options: Because of their iniquity—they even slew a prophet of God
Because they were too few to defend themselves
Because they refused to pay taxes
Because they had no king

7.title: The unnamed prophet
7.text: The prophet Limhi says his people slew—who testified that Christ is God and would come to earth—is Abinadi, whose full story, and that of his convert Alma, comes in the chapters that follow.

7.title: Sow filthiness, reap the whirlwind
7.text: Limhi quotes the Lord's warning that those who ‘sow filthiness shall reap the whirlwind’ and ‘the east wind’—farming imagery for how sin brings swift, self-inflicted destruction.

7.template: turn to the Lord with full purpose of {0}

8.question: What did Ammon explain to king Limhi's people?
options: The last words king Benjamin had taught—his great sermon
How to build a temple
The location of hidden gold
A treaty with the Lamanites

8.title: Reuniting two peoples
8.text: Ammon's arrival reunites two long-separated Nephite groups: he brings Limhi's people the words of king Benjamin and news of Zarahemla, while learning their story of bondage.

8.question: What did Limhi's lost search party discover in the wilderness?
options: A land covered with bones and ruins, and twenty-four gold plates
The land of Zarahemla
A new sea route home
A peaceful Lamanite city

8.title: The twenty-four gold plates
8.text: The plates the search party found are the record of the Jaredites—the people whose ruins they saw. King Mosiah will later translate them; that account becomes the book of Ether.

8.question: What did king Limhi learn about a seer?
options: A seer is greater than a prophet
A seer cannot see the future
A seer must be a king
A seer needs no gift from God

8.title: The interpreters
8.text: Ammon describes ‘interpreters’—sacred instruments prepared by God so a seer can translate ancient records. Only one commanded of God may use them; such a person ‘is called seer.’

8.question: How does Ammon describe a seer?
options: A revelator and a prophet, with a great gift from God
Merely a record-keeper
A warrior chosen by the people
One who interprets dreams only

8.template: a seer can know of things which are {0}, and also of things which are to come
a wild flock which fleeth from the {0}

9.title: The record of Zeniff
9.text: Mosiah 9 shifts to a first-person voice: it is the ‘record of Zeniff,’ written generations earlier, which Mormon inserts whole into his abridgment to tell how the colony fell into bondage.

9.template: having been sent as a {0} among the Lamanites

9.question: How does Zeniff describe his own desire for the land of his fathers?
options: He was over-zealous to inherit it
He was indifferent to it
He was forced to claim it by the king
He wished to sell it

9.question: Why did king Laman really yield the land of Lehi-Nephi to Zeniff's people?
options: By cunning, to bring them into bondage later
Out of genuine friendship
Because he feared the Nephites
To repay an old debt

9.title: Neas and sheum
9.text: Zeniff lists their crops as corn, wheat, barley—and ‘neas’ and ‘sheum,’ two grain names left untranslated in the Book of Mormon, thought to be New World plants with no English equivalent.

9.question: How does Zeniff describe the Lamanites who wanted to enslave his people?
options: A lazy and idolatrous people who wished to live off others' labor
A wise and peaceful nation
Skilled farmers and traders
A small and harmless band

9.template: in the {0} of the Lord did we go forth to battle

9.question: How does Zeniff describe the victory over the attacking Lamanites?
options: In the strength of the Lord they prevailed, after crying mightily to Him
They won easily by sheer numbers
They paid the Lamanites to leave
They fled and abandoned the city

9.title: In the strength of the Lord
9.text: Earlier ‘slow to remember the Lord,’ Zeniff's people now cry mightily to Him before battle and go forth ‘in the strength of the Lord’—crediting their victory to God, not their own arms.

10.title: Twenty-two years of peace
10.text: Zeniff's people enjoyed twenty-two years of peace and industry: the men tilled the ground and raised grain and fruit, while the women spun and wove fine linen and cloth of every kind.

10.question: After re-establishing the kingdom, what did Zeniff do to protect his people?
options: He made weapons of war and set guards around the land
He built a great temple
He paid tribute to the Lamanites
He moved everyone back to Zarahemla

10.question: What happened after king Laman died?
options: His son stirred the Lamanites to war against Zeniff's people
The Lamanites made a lasting peace
Zeniff's people moved to a new land
The two peoples united under one king

10.template: they were girded with a {0} girdle about their loins
therefore they depended upon their own {0}

10.question: What false tradition did the Lamanites believe about their ancestors?
options: That Nephi had wronged and robbed them, so they hated his children
That they were the firstborn heirs of Egypt
That they had built the city of Jerusalem
That the Nephites were their servants

10.title: The Lamanites' grudge
10.text: Zeniff records the Lamanites' own grievance story—that Nephi had wronged them in the wilderness, on the sea, and in the promised land, and 'robbed' them of the brass plates—an inherited grudge reaching back to 1 Nephi.

10.question: How did the battle against the attacking Lamanites end?
options: Zeniff's people drove them out with great slaughter
Zeniff's people were taken captive
The Lamanites burned the city
Both sides made a treaty

10.question: How does Zeniff end his record?
options: Being old, he conferred the kingdom on one of his sons
He named no successor
He divided the land among his sons
He returned to Zarahemla to die

10.title: Zeniff's heir
10.text: Zeniff hands the kingdom to one of his sons—Noah—closing his own hopeful record just before the account turns to one of the Book of Mormon's most wicked kings.

11.question: How did king Noah differ from his father Zeniff?
options: He did not walk in his father's ways, but in wickedness
He ruled even more righteously
He refused to be king
He gave up all his riches

11.template: he laid a tax of one {0} part of all they possessed

11.title: Ziff
11.text: Among the metals Noah taxed is 'ziff'—a word left untranslated in the Book of Mormon, alongside gold, silver, copper, brass, and iron; it is thought to be a precious metal or alloy with no English name.

11.question: What did king Noah do with the riches from his heavy taxes?
options: Built elegant buildings, a palace, and a throne, and adorned the temple
Gave them to the poor
Bought peace from the Lamanites
Hid them in the wilderness

11.template: a spacious palace, and a {0} in the midst thereof
he placed his heart upon his {0}

11.question: What did king Noah build near the temple to overlook the land?
options: A very high tower
A great wall
A wide harbor
A row of statues

11.template: saying that their {0} could stand against thousands of the Lamanites

11.question: What was Abinadi's first message to the people of king Noah?
options: Wo unto them for their abominations; repent or be visited in anger
That they would soon conquer Zarahemla
That Noah was a righteous king
That the law of Moses was abolished

11.title: Abinadi appears
11.text: The prophet Abinadi enters suddenly, with no genealogy given—he simply 'went forth among them' to declare the Lord's word against king Noah's wickedness.

11.question: What did Abinadi warn would happen unless the people repented?
options: They would be brought into bondage, and none could deliver them but God
Their city would sink into the sea
They would be scattered to other lands
Their king would be replaced

11.question: How did king Noah react to Abinadi's words?
options: He was wroth and commanded that Abinadi be brought to be slain
He repented and humbled himself
He fled into the wilderness
He gave Abinadi half his kingdom

11.template: king Noah {0} his heart against the word of the Lord

11.title: Delivered for now
11.text: When the people first sought to kill Abinadi, 'the Lord delivered him out of their hands'—he would return two years later, this time in disguise.

12.title: Abinadi returns in disguise
12.text: Two years after escaping, Abinadi comes back among the people 'in disguise, that they knew him not'—a bold return to deliver the Lord's word to a people who had hardened their hearts.

12.question: What did Abinadi prophesy would come upon the people for their iniquities?
options: They would be brought into bondage, smitten, driven, and slain
They would become rich and proud
They would be carried back to Jerusalem
They would forget the law of Moses

12.template: shall be valued even as a garment in a hot {0}
I will send forth {0} among them, and it shall smite them

12.question: What did the Lord say would survive even if the people were destroyed?
options: A record they would leave behind, preserved for other nations
Their gold and silver
Their king and his priests
Their flocks and herds

12.template: even as a dry {0} of the field

12.question: How did the priests respond to Abinadi's prophecy before the king?
options: They defended Noah as guiltless and said Abinadi had lied
They begged the king to repent
They set Abinadi free
They agreed with his warning

12.title: Vivid prophetic imagery
12.text: Abinadi's prophecies are full of stark images: Noah's life 'as a garment in a hot furnace,' the people 'as a dry stalk,' and 'as the blossoms of a thistle' driven before the wind.

12.question: What did king Noah do with Abinadi after the priests accused him?
options: Cast him into prison and gathered the priests to counsel about him
Set him free at once
Made him a royal advisor
Sent him back to Zarahemla

12.template: How beautiful upon the {0} are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings

12.question: How did Abinadi answer the priests who asked the meaning of the scripture?
options: He rebuked them: as priests who claim to teach, they should already know
He refused to speak at all
He praised them for their wisdom
He asked the king to explain it

12.title: Abinadi quotes Isaiah
12.text: The priests test Abinadi with a passage from Isaiah—'How beautiful upon the mountains are the feet of him that bringeth good tidings' (Isaiah 52:7–10)—which Abinadi turns back on them for failing to understand it.

12.template: Ye have not applied your hearts to {0}

12.question: What did the priests claim they taught the people?
options: The law of Moses
The traditions of the Lamanites
The words of king Noah
A new gospel of riches

12.question: What did the priests claim about salvation and the law of Moses?
options: That salvation came by the law of Moses
That salvation was impossible
That only the king could be saved
That the law of Moses was a lie

12.template: the commandments which the Lord delivered unto Moses in the mount of {0}

12.title: Reciting the Ten Commandments
12.text: To answer the priests, Abinadi begins reciting the Ten Commandments the Lord gave Moses at Sinai—starting with 'I am the Lord thy God' and 'Thou shalt have no other God before me.'

12.template: Thou shalt not make unto thee any graven {0}

13.template: for God shall {0} you if ye lay your hands upon me

13.question: Why couldn't king Noah's people lay their hands on Abinadi?
options: The Spirit was upon him and his face shone, even as Moses' did
He was surrounded by armed guards
He had hidden among the crowd
The king commanded them to spare him

13.title: A face like Moses'
13.text: As Abinadi spoke, the Spirit of the Lord was upon him and 'his face shone with exceeding luster, even as Moses' did' on Mount Sinai—a sign of divine authority that left his accusers powerless to touch him.

13.question: How did Abinadi know he could finish his message?
options: They had no power to slay him until he was done
The king had promised him safety
The priests agreed to listen
An angel stood beside him

13.template: shall be as a type and a {0} of things which are to come

13.question: What does the commandment against graven images forbid?
options: Making or bowing down to any carved image or likeness
Building any temple at all
Writing on metal plates
Speaking the name of God

13.template: Thou shalt not take the name of the Lord thy God in {0}

13.title: Reciting the Ten Commandments
13.text: Abinadi reads the priests 'the remainder of the commandments of God,' giving the Ten Commandments nearly word for word as in Exodus 20—because, he says, they were 'not written in your hearts.'

13.question: What reason does the commandment give for keeping the sabbath holy?
options: In six days the Lord made heaven and earth, then hallowed the seventh
The Lamanites rested on that day
It was the king's birthday
The harvest required it

13.template: But the {0} day, the sabbath of the Lord thy God

pairs.left: Thou shalt not bear false witness

13.question: After reciting the commandments, what did Abinadi ask the priests?
options: Whether they had taught the people to keep them
Whether they would set him free
Whether the king had repented
Whether they knew his name

13.question: What did Abinadi say about the law of Moses?
options: Keep it for now, but the time will come when it is no longer needed
It was never given by God
It alone can save mankind
It should be replaced with Lamanite custom

13.template: a law of performances and of {0}
there could not any man be saved except it were through the {0} of God

13.question: What did Abinadi say Moses and all the prophets foretold?
options: The coming of the Messiah and that God would redeem his people
The fall of king Noah's palace
A long age of peace
The discovery of gold plates

13.title: Pointing toward Isaiah
13.text: Abinadi ends by asking whether the prophets did not foretell that God himself would be 'oppressed and afflicted'—leading straight into his quotation of Isaiah's prophecy of the suffering Messiah in the next chapter.

14.title: This chapter is Isaiah 53
14.text: Abinadi answers the priests by quoting Isaiah 53 almost word for word—the great 'Suffering Servant' song. He uses it to prove that the prophets foretold a Messiah who would suffer for the people's sins.

14.question: How does Isaiah say people first regarded the suffering servant?
options: They despised him and esteemed him stricken, hiding their faces from him
They crowned him king at once
They followed him in great crowds
They wrote his words in gold

14.template: But he was {0} for our transgressions
All we, like {0}, have gone astray

14.question: How does Isaiah describe the servant in his suffering?
options: Silent—like a lamb to the slaughter, he opened not his mouth
Crying out for vengeance
Calling down fire on his enemies
Fleeing into the wilderness

14.title: Silent before his accusers
14.text: Isaiah's image of the servant who 'opened not his mouth,' like a lamb to the slaughter, was fulfilled when Christ stood silent before those who condemned him.

14.question: What does Isaiah say the servant did for transgressors?
options: He bore the sins of many and made intercession for them
He turned away from them in anger
He demanded payment for their wrongs
He left them to their fate

14.title: A prophecy of the Atonement
14.text: 'By his knowledge shall my righteous servant justify many, for he shall bear their iniquities'—Isaiah describes the very work of the Atonement centuries before Christ was born.

15.question: What did Abinadi teach that God himself would do?
options: Come down among the children of men and redeem his people
Send fire to destroy king Noah
Choose a new king from the priests
Restore the law of Moses forever

15.template: but suffereth himself to be mocked, and {0}, and cast out

15.title: The Father and the Son
15.text: Abinadi teaches that Christ is called both the Father (because conceived by the power of God) and the Son (because of the flesh), yet they are 'one God'—one of the Book of Mormon's deepest passages on the nature of Christ.

15.template: he shall be led, {0}, and slain

15.question: What did Abinadi say God accomplished by breaking the bands of death?
options: He gained victory over death and empowered the Son to intercede for men
He ended the law of Moses
He destroyed the wicked at once
He hid the records away

15.question: Who did Abinadi say are the 'seed' of Christ?
options: All who heed the prophets and believe the Lord would redeem his people
Only the descendants of Lehi
Only the priests of Noah
Only those born after Christ

15.template: And these are they who have published {0}

15.title: The seed of Christ
15.text: Abinadi answers Isaiah's question 'who shall declare his generation?' by teaching that Christ's 'seed' are all the prophets and all who believe in his redemption—they become 'heirs of the kingdom of God.'

15.template: he bringeth to pass the {0} of the dead

15.question: Whose feet does Abinadi say are most beautiful upon the mountains?
options: The Lord's—the founder of peace, who redeemed his people
King Benjamin's
The watchmen of Jerusalem
His own

15.question: What does Abinadi affirm about little children?
options: They also have eternal life
They must wait for baptism to be saved
They share the guilt of their parents
They have no part in the resurrection

15.question: Why can the Lord not redeem those who wilfully rebel and die in sin?
options: Because he cannot deny himself, nor deny justice its claim
Because they were never taught
Because they were too poor
Because no prophet warned them

15.template: shall be declared to every {0}, kindred, tongue, and people

15.title: Closing with Isaiah
15.text: Abinadi ends his sermon with the very words from Isaiah the priests had quoted to test him (Isaiah 52:8–10)—'the Lord hath made bare his holy arm in the eyes of all the nations'—turning their own challenge into a witness of redemption.

15.template: The Lord hath made bare his holy {0} in the eyes of all the nations

16.title: 'See eye to eye'
16.text: Abinadi's last sermon opens with the day when 'every nation, kindred, tongue, and people shall see eye to eye' and confess before God that his judgments are just.

16.template: yea, even that old {0} that did beguile our first parents

16.question: What did Abinadi say about all mankind without redemption?
options: All were lost and would be endlessly lost unless God redeemed them
They could save themselves by the law of Moses
Only the priests were in danger
They were already saved by their birth

16.question: What did Abinadi say would have been impossible if Christ had not risen?
options: There could have been no resurrection
The law of Moses would end
The Lamanites would never repent
King Noah would not be judged

16.template: the {0} of death is swallowed up in Christ
He is the {0} and the life of the world
while the arms of {0} were extended towards them

16.question: According to Abinadi, how can people be saved?
options: Only in and through Christ
By keeping the law alone
By their own good works
By the king's decree

16.title: Sealing his message
16.text: Abinadi's final words sum up everything he taught: 'redemption cometh through Christ the Lord, who is the very Eternal Father'—the truth he would die for moments later.

17.title: The one who believed
17.text: The only priest who believed Abinadi was Alma—a young man who would escape, write down Abinadi's words, and become the founder of the church among the Nephites.

17.question: While hiding from the king, what did the young Alma do?
options: He wrote down all the words Abinadi had spoken
He gathered an army
He fled to the Lamanites
He burned the king's palace

17.question: What did king Noah demand of Abinadi to spare his life?
options: That he take back all the words he had spoken
That he pay a great tribute
That he become a priest of Noah
That he leave the land forever

17.template: I will not {0} the words which I have spoken unto you

17.question: What turned king Noah from releasing Abinadi to having him slain?
options: The priests accused Abinadi of reviling the king
Abinadi attacked the guards
The people demanded his death
An earthquake struck the city

17.template: even the pains of death by {0}

17.title: Death by fire
17.text: Abinadi was 'scourged with faggots'—bundles of burning sticks—and put to death by fire, becoming the first recorded martyr in the Book of Mormon.

17.question: How did Abinadi die?
options: By fire, because he would not deny the commandments of God
Stoned by the crowd
Drowned in the river
Of old age in prison

17.template: having {0} the truth of his words by his death

17.title: A prophecy fulfilled
17.text: Abinadi foretold his killers would one day 'suffer death by fire' as he did—fulfilled when king Noah himself was later burned (Mosiah 19).

18.question: After fleeing king Noah's servants, what did Alma begin to do?
options: Privately teach the words of Abinadi
Raise an army against the king
Build a new temple
Return to confront Noah

18.template: there was in Mormon a fountain of {0} water
willing to bear one another’s {0}, that they may be light

18.question: What did Alma say those who would join God's people must be willing to do?
options: Bear one another's burdens, mourn with those who mourn, and stand as witnesses of God
Give up all their possessions
Take up arms against the king
Live apart from all other people

18.title: The baptismal covenant
18.text: At the waters of Mormon Alma states the baptismal covenant: to bear one another's burdens, mourn with those that mourn, comfort those needing comfort, and stand as witnesses of God 'at all times and in all places.'

18.question: How did the people respond to Alma's invitation to be baptized?
options: They clapped their hands for joy: 'This is the desire of our hearts'
They argued among themselves
They asked for more time to decide
They demanded a sign

18.template: I baptize thee, having {0} from the Almighty God

18.title: Alma baptized himself too
18.text: When Alma baptized Helam, 'both Alma and Helam were buried in the water'—Alma immersed himself as well, an unusual detail of this first baptism.

pairs.right: Former priest of Noah who taught Abinadi's words
What the baptized believers were called

18.template: even one priest to every {0} of their number did he ordain
having their hearts knit together in {0} and in love

18.question: What did Alma command the priests concerning their support?
options: To labor with their own hands, not depend on the people
To collect a tithe of gold
To live in the king's court
To take no thought for food at all

18.question: How did Alma command the people to share their substance?
options: Each according to what he had—more from the abundant, and to him who had not, given
Equally, the same amount from everyone
Only with the priests
Only in times of famine

18.template: how {0} are they to the eyes of them who there came to the knowledge of their Redeemer

18.title: The waters of Mormon
18.text: 'The place of Mormon, the waters of Mormon, the forest of Mormon'—where the church was founded in secret—is remembered as beautiful to all who there 'came to the knowledge of their Redeemer.'

18.question: What did Alma's people do when the king's army came?
options: Took their tents and families and fled into the wilderness
Surrendered to the king
Hid in the waters of Mormon
Fought the army in battle

18.template: they were in number about {0} souls

19.title: Gideon
19.text: Gideon, who here rises against king Noah, becomes a trusted captain and leader; the later Nephite city and valley of Gideon are named after him.

19.question: Who rose up against king Noah and swore to slay him?
options: Gideon, a strong man and enemy of the king
Abinadi the prophet
His son Limhi
A Lamanite spy

19.question: When the Lamanite army appeared, what did king Noah beg Gideon to do?
options: Spare his life
Lead the army into battle
Surrender the city
Hide him in the temple

19.template: as he was about his own {0}

19.question: What did those who stayed with their families do to keep from being killed?
options: Had their fair daughters plead with the Lamanites
Built a wall around the camp
Offered all their gold
Fought to the last man

19.template: they were charmed with the {0} of their women

19.title: A heavy tribute
19.text: To keep their lives, Limhi's people agree to hand over king Noah and pay the Lamanites a yearly tribute of one half of all they possessed.

19.question: How did king Noah die?
options: His own people put him to death by fire
He was slain by the Lamanites
He died of old age in prison
He fled and was never found

19.title: Abinadi's prophecy fulfilled
19.text: King Noah is burned to death by his own people—just as Abinadi prophesied that Noah's life would be 'valued even as a garment in a hot furnace' (Mosiah 12:3).

19.template: returned to the land of Nephi, {0}, because their wives and their children were not slain

options: That his people would not slay them
That he would set them free
That he would become a Nephite
That he would never return

19.question: Who became king of the Nephites in the land of Nephi after Noah?
options: Limhi, the son of Noah
Gideon
Ammon
A Lamanite governor

19.template: continual peace in his kingdom for the space of {0} years

20.question: Why would the priests of king Noah not return to the city of Nephi?
options: They were ashamed and feared the people would slay them
They had been forbidden by the king
They had found a better land
They feared the Lamanites only

20.template: yea, {0} of the daughters of the Lamanites they carried into the wilderness

20.title: The priests' crime
20.text: Noah's fugitive priests kidnap twenty-four Lamanite daughters—an act that nearly destroys Limhi's people, who are wrongly blamed for it.

20.question: Why did the Lamanites attack the people of Limhi?
options: They blamed Limhi's people for their missing daughters
Limhi had stopped paying tribute
Limhi had attacked their cities
They wanted the city of Nephi

20.template: for they fought like {0} for their prey
they exerted themselves and like {0} did they fight

20.question: Why did the Lamanite king say he had broken his oath and attacked?
options: Because Limhi's people had carried away the Lamanite daughters
Because Limhi refused to pay tribute
Because Limhi had insulted him
Because of a drought in the land

20.question: What did Gideon point out to king Limhi about the stolen daughters?
options: It was Noah's priests, hiding in the wilderness, who took them
Lamanite raiders had taken them
No daughters were really missing
The Lamanite king had taken them himself

20.template: it is better that we should be in {0} than that we should lose our lives

20.title: 'The words of Abinadi are fulfilled'
20.text: Gideon recognizes that the bondage Abinadi foretold has come upon them—because the people would not turn from their iniquities and hearken to the Lord.

20.question: How did Limhi's people go out to meet the Lamanite army to make peace?
options: Without arms
With their full army
Behind the wounded Lamanite king
Carrying gifts of gold

options: They had compassion and returned in peace
They attacked at once
They demanded double tribute
They took more captives

21.title: The prophecy of burdens fulfilled
21.text: The Lamanites smite Limhi's people on the cheeks and drive them 'as a dumb ass'—fulfilling Abinadi's prophecy that they would have burdens lashed upon their backs (Mosiah 12:5).

21.question: Why does Mormon say all these afflictions came upon Limhi's people?
options: That the word of the Lord (Abinadi's prophecy) might be fulfilled
Because they had no king
Because they were too few
Because of a famine

21.question: What happened when Limhi's people went to battle against the Lamanites?
options: They were beaten and driven back with great loss
They won an easy victory
The Lamanites fled at once
They made a lasting peace

21.template: there were a great many {0} in the land

21.question: How many times did Limhi's people try to throw off the Lamanites by battle?
options: Three times, each time driven back
Once, and they won
Seven times
They never fought back

21.template: subjecting themselves to the {0} of bondage

21.question: What did king Limhi command because so many men had been slain?
options: That every man help support the widows and their children
That all weapons be destroyed
That the people fast for a week
That tribute be doubled

21.template: unless he took his {0} with him

options: He supposed they were the fugitive priests of Noah
They had attacked his guards

21.template: he was filled with exceedingly great {0}

21.title: A land of dry bones
21.text: Limhi's search party, seeking Zarahemla, instead found a land 'covered with dry bones' and a record on plates of ore—the ruins of the Jaredites, whose story (the book of Ether) Mosiah will translate.

21.question: What did Limhi's lost search party bring back from the destroyed land?
options: A record engraven on plates of ore
A chest of gold
A captive Lamanite king
A map to Zarahemla

21.template: they also did mourn for the death of {0}

21.question: Why were Limhi's people not baptized at this time?
options: No one in the land had authority from God, and Ammon felt unworthy
They did not believe Ammon
The king forbade it
There was no water in the land

21.title: A baptism deferred
21.text: Limhi's people long to be baptized like Alma's followers, but with no one holding authority they wait; their baptism is finally recorded later (Mosiah 25).

21.question: What was now the chief concern of Ammon and Limhi's people?
options: To deliver themselves out of Lamanite bondage
To build a temple
To conquer the Lamanites
To return to the land of their fathers

22.title: The voice of the people
22.text: Before acting, Ammon and king Limhi gather everyone to 'have the voice of the people'—deciding together rather than by royal command alone.

22.template: thinking to deliver themselves out of bondage by the {0}

options: Deliver them out of bondage
Lead them in battle against the Lamanites
Become their new king
Pay their tribute himself

22.question: What was Gideon's plan to escape the Lamanites?
options: Get the guards drunk with extra wine, then slip away by night
Tunnel beneath the city wall
Set fire to the Lamanite camp
Bribe the Lamanite king

22.template: and pay the last tribute of {0} to the Lamanites

22.title: A bloodless escape
22.text: Limhi's people escape without a single battle: Gideon sends the Lamanite guards extra wine, and while they sleep drunk, the whole people slip away by night with their flocks.

22.question: Where did Limhi's people go after escaping the Lamanites?
options: To the land of Zarahemla, joining king Mosiah's people
Back to the land of their fathers
Into a new wilderness to live alone
To the city of Mormon

options: After two days they lost the tracks and were lost in the wilderness
They overtook and captured them
They turned back at the border
They made peace with Mosiah

23.question: Why did Alma and his people flee into the wilderness?
options: Alma was warned the armies of king Noah would come against them
A famine struck the land
The Lamanites demanded tribute
They sought a land of gold

Kings always became wicked
He wished to rule alone as high priest
The Lamanites forbade it

23.template: and I myself was caught in a {0}

23.title: Alma refuses a crown
23.text: Though his people loved him and wanted him as king, Alma refused: the Lord taught that no one should 'esteem one flesh above another.' He warns them to trust no king or teacher who is not a man of God.

23.template: I desire that ye should stand fast in this {0} wherewith ye have been made free

Their king and judge

23.title: The Lord chastens his people
23.text: Mormon notes that the Lord 'seeth fit to chasten his people' and try their patience and faith—even righteous Alma's people would be brought into bondage before being delivered.

To choose him as king

Made Alma their king
Returned to their own land

23.title: The priests now rule
23.text: In a bitter turn, Amulon—a former wicked priest of king Noah—is made a petty king over Alma's righteous people, setting up the persecution of the next chapter.

Their own language

Humble and devout worshippers
A peaceful farming people

24.title: Amulon's grudge
24.text: Amulon recognizes Alma as the very priest who had believed Abinadi and fled king Noah's court—so he singles Alma's people out for harsh persecution.

To destroy the Lamanites at once

To give them a new king

That they should fight the Lamanites

24.title: Two deliverances
24.text: Two escapes stand side by side: Limhi's people got free by their own clever wine ruse, while Alma's humbler people were delivered directly by God—who first made their burdens light, then cast a deep sleep on the Lamanites.

24.question: Why did Alma's people name the valley after Alma?

Because he was made king there

25.title: The people of Zarahemla
25.text: They descend from Mulek—a son of King Zedekiah of Judah who escaped to the New World (Omni 1; Helaman 8). United now under Mosiah, they are all numbered as Nephites.

25.template: the people of Zarahemla, who was a descendant of {0}

options: They were fewer—not half so numerous
They were exactly equal

They had no idea of their numbers

With anger at king Mosiah
With total indifference
They demanded war on the Lamanites

25.title: Seven churches, one church
25.text: King Mosiah authorizes Alma to establish churches throughout Zarahemla—seven in all—each with its own priests and teachers, yet all 'one church, even the church of God.'

25.question: Though there were many congregations, what does Mormon say about them?
options: They were all one church, the church of God

26.title: The rising generation
26.text: A 'rising generation'—too young to grasp king Benjamin's sermon—grows up in unbelief, showing that faith is not inherited but must be embraced by each generation.

26.title: Alma inquires of the Lord
26.text: Faced with a problem he'd never seen, Alma pours out his soul and inquires of the Lord—and receives revelation on how to govern the church, a model of seeking guidance.

26.title: As often as they repent
26.text: The Lord reveals a sweeping promise: 'as often as my people repent will I forgive them'—and commands that they likewise forgive one another, or stand condemned.

27.title: Alma the Younger, the prodigal
27.text: Alma the Younger—named for his father, the high priest—is the Book of Mormon's great prodigal: a 'very wicked' man who, with the sons of Mosiah, sought to destroy the church.

27.title: A father's prayers
27.text: The angel comes to Alma not for his own sake but because his father prayed 'with much faith' for him—one of scripture's strongest examples of the power of a parent's prayers.

27.title: The future missionaries
27.text: The four sons of Mosiah—Ammon, Aaron, Omner, and Himni—are converted with Alma; they will later become the great missionaries to the Lamanites (Alma 17–26).

27.question: What did Alma the Younger and the sons of Mosiah become?

28.title: From vilest sinners to missionaries
28.text: The sons of Mosiah—once the 'vilest of sinners'—are now so changed that they 'could not bear that any human soul should perish,' and beg to preach to their old enemies, the Lamanites.

28.question: Why were the sons of Mosiah so eager to preach to the Lamanites?
options: They could not bear that any human soul should perish
They wanted to rule over them

He forbade them to go

28.title: A king with no heir
28.text: King Mosiah faces a crisis: none of his sons will accept the throne. This sets up his great proposal in the next chapter—to replace kings with judges.

28.question: What did king Mosiah translate for his anxious people?
options: The gold plates found by Limhi's people—the record of a destroyed nation
A new code of laws
The brass plates of the Jews
A letter from the Lamanite king

28.question: What did the translated record reveal?
options: The history of a destroyed people, back to the great tower and the creation of Adam
A map of hidden treasure
The future of the Nephites
A list of Lamanite kings

28.title: The record of the Jaredites
28.text: The gold plates Mosiah translates are the record of the Jaredites, reaching back to the Tower of Babel. That account, abridged by Moroni, becomes the book of Ether.

28.question: To whom did Mosiah finally entrust all the records and the interpreters?

King Limhi

29.title: Asking the voice of the people
29.text: Rather than simply name a successor, Mosiah turns to 'the voice of the people'—beginning a shift from kings toward a system of elected judges.

29.question: Whom did the people first want as their next king?
options: Aaron, son of Mosiah
Alma the Younger
Gideon
Limhi

29.template: Now Aaron had gone up to the land of {0}

29.question: Why was Mosiah reluctant to appoint another king after his sons declined?
options: It could stir up contentions, wars, and bloodshed
No one else was wise enough
The Lamanites forbade it
The law required a son of the king

God judges more harshly
Men cannot read the law
Only God knows the future

29.question: What does Mosiah warn about a single wicked king?
options: He causes much iniquity and great destruction, like king Noah
He can be removed easily
He is still better than judges
He cannot harm a righteous people

29.template: extending the arm of {0} towards them that put their trust in him

29.title: King Noah, the great warning
29.text: Mosiah points to king Noah as the prime example: one wicked king brought destruction and bondage upon a whole people.

29.template: save it be through much contention, and the shedding of much {0}

29.question: How did Mosiah say the people should choose their judges?
options: By the voice of the people
By lot among the priests
By right of birth
By trial of arms

29.title: Government by the voice of the people
29.text: Mosiah's reform rests on 'the voice of the people'—judges chosen by the people and answerable to higher and lower judges, an early form of representative government in scripture.

29.template: ye can cause that they may be judged of a higher {0}
the sins of many people have been caused by the iniquities of their {0}

29.question: What did Mosiah desire for the land?
options: That it be a land of liberty, with every man enjoying his rights alike
That it be ruled by one strong family
That it be divided among the tribes
That it return to having a king

29.template: Therefore they relinquished their desires for a {0}

29.question: Why did the people esteem king Mosiah so greatly?
options: He was no tyrant—he sought no riches, shed no blood, and established peace
He had conquered the Lamanites
He had made everyone wealthy
He had built great cities

29.question: What two offices did Alma the Younger hold?
options: First chief judge and high priest
King and prophet
Captain and scribe
Teacher and judge only

29.template: his father died, being {0} years old
making in the whole, {0} years from the time Lehi left Jerusalem

29.question: What two things ended at the close of the book of Mosiah?
options: The reign of the kings over the Nephites, and the days of Alma, founder of the church
The Lamanite wars and the famine
The plates of brass and the temple
The land of Nephi and the city of Helam

29.title: The end of an era
29.text: The book of Mosiah closes an age: the kings give way to judges, and Alma the elder—founder of the Nephite church—dies. The 'reign of the judges' frames the next book, Alma.
